sources: EMA/CHMP/EWP/QWP/1401/98 Rev. 1 — Guideline on the Investigation of Bioequivalence (EMA, 2010)
EMA/CHMP/BWP/247713/2012 — Guideline on development, production, characterisation and specification for monoclonal antibodies (EMA, 2016)
EMEA/CHMP/BMWP/42832/2005 Rev. 1 — Guideline on similar biological medicinal products containing biotechnology-derived proteins as active substance (EMA, 2014)
EMA/CHMP/BMWP/86289/2010 — Guideline on immunogenicity assessment of therapeutic proteins (EMA, 2017)
EMEA/CHMP/EWP/205921/2005 — Guideline on the evaluation of anticancer medicinal products in man (EMA, 2012)
EMA/CHMP/83561/2005 — Guideline on clinical trials in small populations (EMA, 2006)
EMA/CHMP/QWP/396951/2006 — Guideline on excipients in the label and package leaflet (EMA, 2008)
EMA/541760/2011 — GVP Module I: Pharmacovigilance systems and their quality systems (EMA, 2012)
EMA/838713/2011 Rev 2 — GVP Module V: Risk management systems (EMA, 2014)
EMA/827661/2011 Rev 1 — GVP Module IX: Signal management (EMA, 2012)
PMDA — Application for Accreditation of Foreign Manufacturers (PAL Article 13-3)
Notification 薬食審査発第1127001号 — Carcinogenicity study guideline ICH S1B adaptation (PMDA, 2008)
MHLW/PMSB — Guideline on drug interaction for drug development and appropriate provision of information (2019)
PMDA Research Promotion Overview (2004) — organizational document, no regulatory rules

rules.rule_text: Two medicinal products are bioequivalent if they are pharmaceutically equivalent or pharmaceutical alternatives and their bioavailabilities (rate and extent of absorption) after administration in the same molar dose are similar to such a degree that their effects, with respect to both efficacy and safety, will be essentially the same.
rules.source_quote: Two medicinal products are bioequivalent if they are pharmaceutically equivalent or pharmaceutical alternatives and their bioavailabilities (rate and extent of absorption) after administration in the same molar dose are similar to such a degree that their effects, with respect to both efficacy and safety, will be essentially the same.
rules.exceptions: Not applicable as foundational definition

rules.rule_text: The 90% confidence interval for the ratio of the population geometric means (Test/Reference) for AUC and Cmax must lie within the acceptance interval of 80.00-125.00% for the primary PK parameters.
rules.source_quote: The 90% confidence interval for this measure has to be calculated and has to lie within an acceptance interval of 80.00-125.00%.
rules.exceptions: Narrow therapeutic index drugs may use tighter limits (90.00-111.11%). Highly variable drugs may use widened limits up to 69.84-143.19% for Cmax only if justified.

rules.rule_text: For highly variable drugs (intra-subject CV ≥30%), the acceptance interval for Cmax may be widened to a maximum of 69.84-143.19%, provided this is prospectively defined in the protocol and the scaling is based on the within-subject variability of the reference product.
rules.source_quote: In these cases widening of the acceptance interval for Cmax is acceptable if it is based on the within-subject variability observed with the reference product in a replicate design study... The acceptance interval for Cmax can be widened to a maximum of 69.84-143.19%.
rules.exceptions: Widening applies only to Cmax; AUC acceptance interval remains 80.00-125.00%. Widening not applicable if the drug is narrow therapeutic index.

rules.rule_text: For narrow therapeutic index drugs, the acceptance interval must be tightened to 90.00-111.11% for AUC, and a similar tightening applied to Cmax.
rules.source_quote: For NTI drugs the acceptance interval should be tightened to 90.00-111.11% for AUCt/AUC∞ (or AUCt when this is the primary parameter) and for Cmax.
rules.exceptions: Product-specific guidance may override; tightened interval must be defined prospectively.

rules.rule_text: Bioequivalence studies must generally be conducted as single-dose, two-period, two-sequence, crossover studies under fasting conditions, unless fasting is not acceptable for reasons of safety.
rules.source_quote: Generally, a single-dose, two-period, two-sequence crossover study is recommended.
rules.exceptions: Modified-release formulations, fixed combinations, and drugs with long half-lives may require fed state or parallel design.

rules.rule_text: The reference product in bioequivalence studies must be the innovator product (originator) authorized in the EU or, where not available in the EU, the innovator product authorized in a country with a comparable regulatory framework.
rules.source_quote: The Reference Product should in principle be the innovator medicinal product as authorised in the EU.
rules.exceptions: Where innovator is not available in EU, a product authorized in a country with a comparable regulatory framework may be used.

rules.rule_text: A minimum of 12 evaluable subjects is required for a bioequivalence study; sample size must be prospectively determined based on a power calculation using an estimate of intra-subject variability.
rules.source_quote: A minimum of 12 evaluable subjects should be included. The number of subjects required depends on the intra-subject variability.
rules.exceptions: Replicate designs may allow fewer subjects per period; rare disease products may accept smaller samples with justification.

rules.rule_text: The washout period between treatments in a crossover bioequivalence study must be at least 5 times the terminal half-life of the medicinal product.
rules.source_quote: The washout period should be at least 5 times the terminal half-life of the medicinal product.
rules.exceptions: Endogenous substances may require different approaches; pre-dose sampling to establish baseline is recommended.

rules.rule_text: For oral modified-release products, bioequivalence must be demonstrated in both fasting and fed conditions. Failure to demonstrate bioequivalence under both conditions results in a refused application.
rules.source_quote: For modified release products both fasting and fed state studies are generally required.
rules.exceptions: If the product is only to be used in fasting or fed state as clinically specified, only the relevant condition need be studied.

rules.rule_text: Biowaivers based on the Biopharmaceutics Classification System (BCS) are acceptable for immediate-release solid oral dosage forms if the drug substance is BCS Class I (high solubility, high permeability) and dissolution is rapid (≥85% in 30 minutes at pH 1.2, 4.5, and 6.8).
rules.source_quote: A biowaiver can be granted based on the BCS if the drug substance belongs to BCS class I, i.e., it is a high solubility and high permeability compound, and the medicinal product shows rapid dissolution.
rules.exceptions: BCS Class III biowaivers may be acceptable under specific conditions. Narrow therapeutic index drugs are excluded from biowaivers.

rules.rule_text: For fed-state bioequivalence studies, subjects must consume a high-fat, high-calorie test meal (approximately 800-1000 kcal, with 50% derived from fat) starting 30 minutes before dosing.
rules.source_quote: A high-fat (approximately 50 percent of total caloric content of the meal) and high-calorie (approximately 800 to 1000 calories) meal is recommended.
rules.exceptions: The test meal composition may be adjusted for pediatric populations or patients unable to consume such meals.

rules.rule_text: Pharmacokinetic analysis for bioequivalence must use a non-compartmental approach; model-dependent methods are not acceptable as the primary analysis.
rules.source_quote: The primary pharmacokinetic parameters should be obtained by non-compartmental analysis.
rules.exceptions: Population PK or PBPK may be used as supportive analyses but cannot replace the primary non-compartmental analysis.

rules.rule_text: The primary structure of a biosimilar monoclonal antibody must be confirmed by peptide mapping and mass spectrometry; confirmation of disulfide bond assignment is required.
rules.source_quote: The primary structure should be confirmed by peptide mapping. Disulfide bond assignment should be confirmed.
rules.exceptions: Well-characterized legacy products may use orthogonal methods where mass spectrometry was not available at time of original approval.

rules.rule_text: Glycosylation profile must be fully characterized including the nature and distribution of N-glycans and O-glycans; the extent of fucosylation, galactosylation, sialylation, and mannose content must be determined.
rules.source_quote: The glycan profile should be characterised in detail including the nature and the distribution of the N- and O-glycan chains. The extent of glycosylation should be determined.
rules.exceptions: Non-glycosylated mAbs are exempt from glycosylation characterization.

rules.rule_text: Aggregate content must be characterized and controlled; high molecular weight species (aggregates) must be quantified using multiple orthogonal methods (e.g., SEC, AUC, DLS). Aggregates above threshold must be subject to specification and batch release testing.
rules.source_quote: The aggregate content should be determined using orthogonal methods. Aggregates above defined limits should be controlled by specifications.
rules.exceptions: Products with historically demonstrated low aggregation may use a single validated method if supported by comparability data.

rules.rule_text: Process-related impurities (host cell proteins, host cell DNA, residual protein A, cell culture media components) must be characterized, and limits justified based on safety assessment. Host cell DNA must be controlled to ≤10 ng per dose or risk-justified limit.
rules.source_quote: Limits for host cell DNA should be justified. Process-related impurities such as host cell proteins... should be characterised and controlled.
rules.exceptions: For well-established manufacturing processes with long safety records, historical data may support less stringent characterization.

rules.rule_text: The reference product for biosimilar comparability exercises must be the EU-authorized originator product. Data generated using non-EU originator are acceptable only as bridging data, not as standalone pivotal comparability data.
rules.source_quote: The Reference Medicinal Product should be the originator product authorised in the EU.
rules.exceptions: Bridging studies using non-EU originator (e.g., US or JP-licensed version) are acceptable to supplement EU comparability data.

rules.rule_text: A comparability exercise between the biosimilar and the reference product must be performed at all levels: physicochemical, biological/functional, and clinical (PK/PD and safety/efficacy). The quality comparability exercise is the foundation and must precede clinical comparability.
rules.source_quote: A stepwise comparability exercise should be conducted including physicochemical and biological characterisation and functional testing, followed by clinical pharmacology and clinical studies.
rules.exceptions: Abbreviated clinical programs may be acceptable if quality and non-clinical comparability is robust.

rules.rule_text: Biological activity (potency) must be assessed using cell-based assays that are relevant to the mechanism of action; receptor binding or enzyme-linked assays alone are insufficient if cell-based assays are feasible.
rules.source_quote: Relevant cell-based assays should be used to assess biological activity. Binding assays alone are not sufficient if cell-based assays can be established.
rules.exceptions: Where no cell-based assay is feasible (e.g., no known cell line expressing the target), well-characterized binding assays may be accepted with justification.

rules.rule_text: For antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC) and complement-dependent cytotoxicity (CDC) mechanisms, Fc-mediated effector functions must be characterized and controlled in the specification.
rules.source_quote: Where the mechanism of action includes ADCC or CDC, these functional properties should be characterised and the relevant tests included in the specification.
rules.exceptions: If the clinical mechanism of action has been demonstrated to be independent of ADCC/CDC (e.g., IgG4 antibodies), Fc function characterization may be reduced.

rules.rule_text: A similar biological medicinal product (biosimilar) must demonstrate similarity in terms of quality, safety, and efficacy to the reference biological product through a comprehensive comparability exercise; it cannot rely solely on its own preclinical and clinical data.
rules.source_quote: An application for a similar biological medicinal product shall contain the results of appropriate preclinical tests or clinical trials relating to this condition.
rules.exceptions: Where switching between originator and biosimilar is concerned, additional pharmacovigilance data may be required.

rules.rule_text: Extrapolation of indications from the reference product to the biosimilar is permissible only when similarity has been demonstrated and the mechanism of action is the same across indications, or scientifically justified.
rules.source_quote: Extrapolation of clinical data to other indications of the reference product is possible if similarity has been demonstrated and there is a scientific justification.
rules.exceptions: Each indication extrapolation requires case-by-case justification; extrapolation is not automatic even when overall biosimilarity is demonstrated.

rules.rule_text: The pharmacokinetic comparability study must demonstrate equivalence of AUC and Cmax within a predefined equivalence margin, typically 80-125%, using a single-dose, crossover or parallel design in a representative population.
rules.source_quote: Pharmacokinetic studies should demonstrate equivalence of AUC and Cmax. The acceptance criteria should normally be 80-125%.
rules.exceptions: Where single-dose crossover is not feasible due to immunogenicity, parallel group design may be used.

rules.rule_text: Immunogenicity of the biosimilar must be compared to that of the reference product in a comparative clinical study of adequate duration; the study must include assessment of anti-drug antibodies (ADA) including neutralizing antibodies.
rules.source_quote: The immunogenicity of the biosimilar should be compared with that of the reference product. Anti-drug antibody responses including neutralising antibodies should be assessed.
rules.exceptions: Highly purified products with demonstrated non-immunogenicity may have a reduced immunogenicity program, with post-marketing surveillance substituted.

rules.rule_text: A risk management plan (RMP) is mandatory for biosimilars, with particular attention to immunogenicity monitoring in the post-marketing setting.
rules.source_quote: A risk management plan should be provided and immunogenicity should be followed in the post-authorisation phase.
rules.exceptions: The scope of the RMP may be calibrated based on the risk profile demonstrated during the comparability exercise.

rules.rule_text: Switching studies or interchangeability data are not required at the time of initial EU authorization; however, if switching data are generated, they must be included in the RMP and pharmacovigilance plan.
rules.source_quote: Switching studies are not required for the purpose of authorisation in the EU, but if data are generated they should be included in the pharmacovigilance plan.
rules.exceptions: National regulations within EU Member States may impose additional requirements on substitution/interchangeability.

rules.rule_text: An immunogenicity risk assessment must be provided for all therapeutic proteins as part of the marketing authorization application, identifying risk factors for immunogenicity including product-related, patient-related, and disease-related factors.
rules.source_quote: The risk of immunogenicity is influenced by product-related, patient-related and disease-related factors which should all be considered in the risk assessment.
rules.exceptions: Well-established biological medicines with extensive post-marketing safety data may rely on existing class-level immunogenicity data.

rules.rule_text: Anti-drug antibody (ADA) assay must be a validated, drug-tolerant assay with a sensitivity of at least 100 ng/mL equivalent of positive control antibody. A tiered testing strategy (screening, confirmation, titration, neutralization) is required.
rules.source_quote: The ADA assay should be drug-tolerant and validated. A tiered testing approach is recommended: screening, confirmatory, and where appropriate, neutralising antibody assay.
rules.exceptions: For products where drug interference cannot be overcome, alternative assay strategies with appropriate justification may be accepted.

rules.rule_text: Neutralizing antibody (NAb) assay must be performed for all therapeutic proteins where ADA response could potentially affect efficacy or safety; both cell-based and non-cell-based assays must be considered.
rules.source_quote: Neutralising antibody assays should be performed for all therapeutic proteins where the immune response could affect clinical outcome.
rules.exceptions: For proteins with endogenous counterparts where NAb could cause serious adverse effects (e.g., pure red cell aplasia for EPO), NAb testing is mandatory.

rules.rule_text: Immunogenicity data must be collected at defined time points throughout the clinical development program and reported in the clinical study reports; the duration of follow-up must be sufficient to detect delayed responses.
rules.source_quote: Immunogenicity data should be collected at pre-specified time points throughout clinical development. Follow-up should be long enough to detect delayed immune responses.
rules.exceptions: For single-dose products, shorter follow-up may be acceptable; the duration must be justified based on the half-life and mechanism.

rules.rule_text: The clinical consequences of immunogenicity (impact on PK, efficacy, and safety including cross-reactivity with endogenous counterparts) must be characterized and reported. ADA status must be correlated with clinical outcomes.
rules.source_quote: The clinical consequences of immunogenicity should be characterised including the impact on pharmacokinetics, efficacy, and safety.
rules.exceptions: For products without endogenous counterparts, cross-reactivity assessment may be waived.

rules.rule_text: For therapeutic proteins with known endogenous counterparts (e.g., erythropoietin, growth hormone), cross-reactivity of ADA with the endogenous protein must be assessed due to the risk of serious adverse events such as pure red cell aplasia.
rules.source_quote: For therapeutic proteins that have an endogenous counterpart, the potential for cross-reactive antibodies against the endogenous protein should be assessed.
rules.exceptions: Products where the endogenous counterpart is structurally unrelated or where cross-reactivity is immunologically impossible may waive this requirement.

rules.rule_text: Immunogenicity must be monitored in the post-authorization phase as part of the Risk Management Plan (RMP); the protocol for post-marketing immunogenicity surveillance must be submitted and approved at authorization.
rules.source_quote: Post-authorisation immunogenicity monitoring should be described in the Risk Management Plan.
rules.exceptions: The scope and duration of post-marketing immunogenicity surveillance may be reduced for products with extensive real-world safety data.

rules.rule_text: Comparability of immunogenicity risk must be established after any manufacturing changes; a dedicated immunogenicity bridging study may be required if the change could affect immunogenicity determinants (e.g., glycosylation changes, new impurities).
rules.source_quote: Comparability exercises following manufacturing changes should include an assessment of potential changes in immunogenicity.
rules.exceptions: Minor changes with no impact on the protein structure or impurity profile may not require immunogenicity bridging.

rules.rule_text: Patients switching between originator and biosimilar, or between different biosimilars, must be monitored for changes in immunogenicity; if switching data are generated they must be submitted to competent authorities.
rules.source_quote: Patients who switch between originator and biosimilar or between biosimilars should be monitored for immunogenicity changes.
rules.exceptions: National health authority guidance on interchangeability takes precedence; monitoring requirements may differ by Member State.

rules.rule_text: Overall survival (OS) is the preferred primary endpoint for regulatory approval of anticancer drugs in the curative or life-extending setting; surrogate endpoints (PFS, ORR) may be acceptable where OS is not feasible but must be validated surrogates.
rules.source_quote: Overall survival is the most clinically relevant endpoint and should be used where feasible. Surrogate endpoints may be acceptable if they are validated surrogates for overall survival.
rules.exceptions: In settings with curative intent and long follow-up, event-free survival or disease-free survival may be acceptable primary endpoints.

rules.rule_text: Progression-free survival (PFS) is an acceptable primary endpoint only if the magnitude of the PFS benefit is clinically meaningful and the effect on subsequent lines of therapy and quality of life is adequately characterized.
rules.source_quote: PFS may be considered an acceptable primary endpoint if the magnitude of the effect is considered clinically relevant and if the effect on post-progression therapy and quality of life is characterised.
rules.exceptions: In rare cancers or conditions where OS is practically impossible to demonstrate, PFS may be accepted without post-progression characterization.

rules.rule_text: Early phase (Phase I/II) anticancer trials should use dose-escalation designs; the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) or optimal biological dose (OBD) must be defined before initiating pivotal trials.
rules.source_quote: Early clinical trials should define the maximum tolerated dose or optimal biological dose before initiation of pivotal studies.
rules.exceptions: Targeted therapies and immunotherapies where MTD is not reached may proceed to pivotal trials based on OBD with justification.

rules.rule_text: Randomized controlled trials with an active comparator are the standard for demonstrating efficacy of new anticancer drugs; single-arm trials may be acceptable only for orphan cancers, highly refractory populations, or when a randomized trial is ethically infeasible.
rules.source_quote: Randomised controlled trials with an active comparator represent the standard for demonstrating efficacy. Single-arm trials may be acceptable in limited circumstances.
rules.exceptions: Accelerated approval based on single-arm data may be granted with a confirmatory trial commitment.

rules.rule_text: Health-related quality of life (HRQoL) data must be collected in all pivotal anticancer trials using a validated patient-reported outcome instrument; HRQoL results must be included in the SmPC and European Public Assessment Report (EPAR).
rules.source_quote: Health-related quality of life data should be collected in all pivotal studies and the results presented in the EPAR.
rules.exceptions: For very short-duration treatments or situations where HRQoL data collection is not feasible (e.g., acute toxic settings), alternatives may be accepted.

rules.rule_text: For targeted oncology therapies with a predictive biomarker, the pivotal trial must enroll only the biomarker-defined population or must include a pre-specified analysis demonstrating superior treatment effects in the biomarker-positive subgroup.
rules.source_quote: For therapies targeting a specific molecular alteration, the pivotal trial should be conducted in the biomarker-positive population or include a prospective subgroup analysis.
rules.exceptions: Basket trials or umbrella trials in rare mutations may use adaptive designs with biomarker enrichment.

rules.rule_text: Combination anticancer regimens must demonstrate the contribution of each component; if a new drug is added to an established backbone, a factorial or add-on design must justify that each component contributes to efficacy.
rules.source_quote: For combination regimens, the contribution of each component should be demonstrated. If a new drug is added to an established regimen, the contribution of the new drug should be justified.
rules.exceptions: Where clinical or ethical reasons prevent factorial testing, mechanistic justification and non-clinical data may partially substitute.

rules.rule_text: For anticancer therapies in pediatric populations, a Paediatric Investigation Plan (PIP) must be agreed with the EMA Paediatric Committee (PDCO) before initiating pivotal trials, unless a waiver or deferral is granted.
rules.source_quote: A Paediatric Investigation Plan should be agreed with the PDCO before pivotal studies are initiated, unless a waiver or deferral applies.
rules.exceptions: Waivers are granted for diseases not occurring in children; deferrals are available where adult data must be obtained first.

rules.rule_text: Anticancer immunotherapies must characterize the durability of response; long-term follow-up plans must be incorporated into the trial design with pre-specified time points for landmark survival analyses.
rules.source_quote: For immunotherapies, the durability of response should be characterised. Long-term follow-up should be planned prospectively.
rules.exceptions: Where immunotherapy is used in short-course adjuvant settings, landmark analyses at 3 and 5 years may substitute for continuous follow-up.

rules.rule_text: Response criteria for solid tumors must use RECIST 1.1 as the standard; alternative response criteria (iRECIST, WHO) may be used as secondary endpoints but RECIST 1.1 results must be provided as the primary efficacy measure.
rules.source_quote: RECIST 1.1 criteria should be used as the standard for response assessment in solid tumours.
rules.exceptions: Hematological malignancies use disease-specific response criteria; iRECIST may be used as co-primary endpoint for immunotherapy if prospectively defined.

rules.rule_text: For accelerated approval (conditional marketing authorization in the EU) based on surrogate endpoints, the applicant must commit to post-authorization confirmatory studies demonstrating overall survival or other clinical benefit; the commitment must be a specific condition of the marketing authorization.
rules.source_quote: Conditional marketing authorisation may be granted on the basis of comprehensive data which indicate that the benefit-risk balance is positive... The applicant must commit to confirmatory post-authorisation studies.
rules.exceptions: Confirmatory study timelines must be agreed with EMA at the time of conditional approval; failure to complete results in withdrawal of authorization.

rules.rule_text: For diseases affecting small populations, conventional statistical significance thresholds (p<0.05) may not be achievable; sponsors must pre-specify alternative decision criteria including Bayesian approaches, adaptive designs, or extrapolation from related populations.
rules.source_quote: In small populations it may not be possible to achieve conventional statistical significance. Alternative approaches such as Bayesian methods, adaptive designs, or extrapolation from related populations may be considered.
rules.exceptions: Products designated as orphan medicinal products may benefit from reduced development requirements under Regulation (EC) No 141/2000.

rules.rule_text: Extrapolation of efficacy data from a larger related population to the small target population is acceptable if the disease mechanism, patient characteristics, and drug mechanism of action are sufficiently similar; the degree of similarity must be formally justified.
rules.source_quote: Extrapolation of data from a larger related population may be acceptable if similarity of disease mechanism, patient characteristics and drug action can be justified.
rules.exceptions: Extrapolation from adults to pediatric populations is governed by the Paediatric Regulation; extrapolation across ethnic groups must also consider ICH E5 guidance.

rules.rule_text: N-of-1 trials and single case studies may provide supportive evidence in very small populations where group studies are infeasible, but cannot constitute standalone pivotal evidence for marketing authorization.
rules.source_quote: N-of-1 trials may provide useful information in very small populations but cannot alone constitute pivotal evidence.
rules.exceptions: In ultra-rare diseases where N-of-1 is the only feasible design, a comprehensive totality of evidence approach may be acceptable.

rules.rule_text: Adaptive trial designs with pre-specified adaptation rules are encouraged in small populations; all adaptations must be described in the protocol and the Type I error must be controlled.
rules.source_quote: Adaptive designs are particularly relevant in small populations. Pre-specified adaptation rules must be described in the protocol and type I error must be controlled.
rules.exceptions: Unplanned adaptations (operational adaptations) must be distinguished from pre-specified statistical adaptations and are not subject to alpha control.

rules.rule_text: Patient registries must be established for diseases affecting small populations to support post-authorization data collection; the registry protocol must be submitted as part of the RMP.
rules.source_quote: Patient registries should be established for diseases in small populations to support post-authorisation data collection.
rules.exceptions: Where a suitable registry already exists (academic or disease-specific), applicants may join existing registries rather than establishing new ones.

rules.rule_text: Cross-over designs are preferred in small populations to maximize information per patient; however, adequate washout must be ensured and carry-over effects must be addressed in the statistical analysis plan.
rules.source_quote: Crossover designs are preferred in small populations. Carry-over effects must be addressed in the analysis plan.
rules.exceptions: Crossover is not appropriate where the disease condition changes substantially over time or where treatment effects are irreversible.

rules.rule_text: All excipients present in the formulation must be listed in the product label (Summary of Product Characteristics) with their quantitative composition; excipients with known action or effect must be specifically labeled with safety information.
rules.source_quote: All excipients should be listed in the SPC with their quantitative composition. Excipients with known action or effect should be specifically labeled.
rules.exceptions: Excipients present in quantities below threshold limits defined in the EMA excipients guideline may be exempt from quantitative disclosure.

rules.rule_text: Products containing benzyl alcohol must carry the warning: 'Contains benzyl alcohol. Not recommended for use in neonates and infants (up to 3 years old).' Where amounts exceed 90 mg/kg/day in neonates, use is contraindicated.
rules.source_quote: Products containing benzyl alcohol should carry the following statement: 'Contains benzyl alcohol. Not recommended for use in neonates and infants (up to 3 years old).'
rules.exceptions: Products for adult use only where no pediatric exposure is possible may have a modified warning.

rules.rule_text: Products containing lactose must include information on the amount of lactose per dose unit in the label; patients with rare hereditary galactose intolerance must be warned not to take the product.
rules.source_quote: Products containing lactose should include a statement regarding the lactose content. Patients with rare hereditary galactose intolerance should be warned.
rules.exceptions: Products with very low lactose content (below threshold causing reactions in lactose-intolerant patients) may have simplified labeling.

rules.rule_text: Products containing azo colorants (tartrazine, sunset yellow, carmoisine, etc.) must label these colorants by name as they may cause allergic reactions in susceptible patients, particularly those who are aspirin-sensitive.
rules.source_quote: Azo colorants... may cause allergic reactions including asthma... the names of these colorants should be specified in the label.
rules.exceptions: Products where the colorant is present only in the coating and is not bioavailable may have reduced labeling requirements.

rules.rule_text: Products containing propylene glycol must label the amount present per dose; if the amount exceeds established safety thresholds (particularly in neonates and children), specific warnings and contraindications must be included in the SmPC.
rules.source_quote: Products containing propylene glycol should state the amount per dose. Safety warnings should be included where amounts exceed established thresholds.
rules.exceptions: Topical products where systemic absorption of propylene glycol is negligible may be exempt from dose-based threshold calculations.

rules.rule_text: Each marketing authorization holder (MAH) must have a Qualified Person Responsible for Pharmacovigilance (QPPV) who permanently resides and operates in the EU/EEA. The QPPV must be a natural person (not a corporate entity).
rules.source_quote: The QPPV shall be established in the Union. The QPPV shall be a natural person.
rules.exceptions: During temporary absence, a designated back-up must assume QPPV responsibilities; the back-up must also reside in the EU/EEA.

rules.rule_text: Each pharmacovigilance system may have only one QPPV; where an MAH holds multiple marketing authorizations, a single QPPV may cover all products provided that person has oversight of all products.
rules.source_quote: Each pharmacovigilance system shall have only one QPPV.
rules.exceptions: Large MAHs with diversified product portfolios may have deputy QPPVs with defined responsibilities, but only one is the designated QPPV.

rules.rule_text: The QPPV must be available 24 hours a day, 7 days a week as a point of contact for pharmacovigilance matters for competent authorities; a designated point of contact must be available when the QPPV is temporarily unavailable.
rules.source_quote: The QPPV shall be accessible on a 24-hour basis... and shall be the contact point for competent authorities.
rules.exceptions: Back-up procedures must be formally documented in the PSMF.

rules.rule_text: The Pharmacovigilance System Master File (PSMF) must be maintained and kept up to date at all times; it must be made available to competent authorities on request within 7 days (or immediately in urgent cases).
rules.source_quote: The PSMF shall be permanently available and shall be provided to competent authorities upon request.
rules.exceptions: Location of the PSMF must be stated in the marketing authorization application; the PSMF may be held electronically.

rules.rule_text: The PSMF must be retained for at least 5 years after termination of the pharmacovigilance system; individual product pharmacovigilance records must be retained for at least 10 years after expiry of the marketing authorization.
rules.source_quote: The PSMF shall be retained for at least 5 years after the termination of the pharmacovigilance system... product safety data shall be retained for at least 10 years after the expiry of the marketing authorisation. [IR Art 12(2)]
rules.exceptions: Electronic retention systems must meet applicable data integrity and archive standards.

rules.rule_text: Risk-based quality audits of the pharmacovigilance system must be conducted; audits must be documented and findings addressed. The audit program must be described in the PSMF.
rules.source_quote: Risk-based quality audits of the pharmacovigilance system shall be conducted. [IR Art 13(1)]
rules.exceptions: The frequency and scope of audits are determined by the risk profile; minimum audit frequency is not specified but must be justified.

rules.rule_text: Even when pharmacovigilance tasks are delegated to a third party (contract), the MAH retains full legal responsibility for pharmacovigilance; subcontracting does not transfer regulatory liability.
rules.source_quote: The marketing authorisation holder retains responsibility for pharmacovigilance activities even where those activities are carried out by a third party. [IR Art 6(1)]
rules.exceptions: Subcontracting agreements must be documented; the QPPV must have oversight of all contracted PV activities.

rules.rule_text: The MAH must submit adverse reaction reports to EudraVigilance within legally mandated timelines (15 calendar days for serious expedited reports, 90 days for non-serious); all ICSRs must be submitted electronically in the E2B(R3) format.
rules.source_quote: The marketing authorisation holder shall submit adverse reaction information to EudraVigilance within the legal timelines. [IR Art 11(c)]
rules.exceptions: Expedited reporting obligations may differ for clinical trial ICSRs (SUSARs); national competent authority requirements may apply additionally.

rules.rule_text: Back-up procedures for the QPPV must be formally documented and regularly tested; the identity of the back-up QPPV must be documented in the PSMF.
rules.source_quote: Back-up procedures shall be in place for situations where the QPPV is temporarily unavailable. [IR Art 2(1)(d)]
rules.exceptions: For small companies with a single QPPV, documented deputy arrangements with a named contact are mandatory.

rules.rule_text: The pharmacovigilance quality system must include standard operating procedures for all pharmacovigilance activities; SOPs must be reviewed and updated at regular intervals.
rules.source_quote: Standard operating procedures shall cover all pharmacovigilance activities and shall be maintained and updated.
rules.exceptions: Frequency of SOP review should be risk-based; major regulatory or procedural changes trigger mandatory SOP review.

rules.rule_text: All new marketing authorization applications must be accompanied by a Risk Management Plan (RMP); submission of an RMP is mandatory and cannot be waived for new active substances.
rules.source_quote: An application for a new marketing authorisation shall include a risk management system... [DIR Art 8(3)(iaa)]
rules.exceptions: Generic and hybrid applications are generally exempt unless risk concerns are identified; well-established use applications follow specific guidance.

rules.rule_text: The RMP must be submitted as a single eCTD document (module 1.8.2) and structured according to the mandatory 7-part RMP template (Parts I-VII) as specified in GVP Module V Annex I.
rules.source_quote: The RMP shall be submitted as a single eCTD document and shall be structured in accordance with the template in Annex I of GVP Module V.
rules.exceptions: Legacy products not originally submitted via eCTD may use transitional submission formats as agreed with the relevant regulatory authority.

rules.rule_text: Module SVI (Additional EU requirements for safety specification) of the RMP must address the potential for the medicine to be misused for illegal purposes, including potential for abuse, dependence, and diversion.
rules.source_quote: Module SVI shall address the potential for off-label use, misuse, abuse, medication errors and (for relevant products) the potential for illegal use. [DIR Art 71(2)]
rules.exceptions: Products with no abuse potential (e.g., parenteral biologics with no street value) may address this section briefly with justification.

rules.rule_text: The RMP summary (Part VI) must be made publicly available in the EU; it must be written in plain language accessible to patients and the general public.
rules.source_quote: The risk management plan summary shall be made publicly available. [REG Art 26(1)(c)]
rules.exceptions: Information classified as commercially confidential may be redacted from the public version; a clear indication of redactions must be provided.

rules.rule_text: The QPPV must review and approve the RMP before submission; evidence of QPPV review and approval must be documented and available on request.
rules.source_quote: The QPPV shall review and approve the RMP before submission.
rules.exceptions: For time-critical regulatory submissions, a documented process for interim QPPV approval may be acceptable.

rules.rule_text: The list of safety concerns in the RMP must be reviewed at 5-year renewal of the marketing authorization and at the first PSUR submitted after renewal; the review must consider whether concerns should be removed, downgraded, or new concerns added.
rules.source_quote: The list of safety concerns shall be reviewed at the time of 5-year renewal and at the first PSUR after renewal.
rules.exceptions: PSURs submitted as part of PSUSA procedure cannot include standalone RMP updates; these must be submitted separately.

rules.rule_text: When a validated signal requires a labeling variation, the variation must be submitted within 3 months for important identified or potential risks, or within 6 months for non-important risks.
rules.source_quote: For validated signals requiring variations: important risks require submission within 3 months; non-important risks within 6 months.
rules.exceptions: Where the variation requires extensive data collection, a timetable extension may be agreed with the competent authority.

rules.rule_text: RMP Annex 1 (summary of safety concerns) must be submitted in XML format within the specified timescale after the Commission decision on the marketing authorization.
rules.source_quote: RMP Annex 1 shall be submitted in XML format within the specified timescale following the Commission decision.
rules.exceptions: Timescales are product-specific and defined in the Commission decision or by EMA guidance.

rules.rule_text: RMP updates cannot be submitted as part of a Periodic Safety Update Single Assessment (PSUSA); RMP updates triggered by PSUSA findings must be submitted as a standalone variation.
rules.source_quote: RMP updates cannot be submitted with PSURs in the context of PSUSA.
rules.exceptions: Minor corrections to the RMP identified during PSUSA may be incorporated at the next scheduled RMP update.

rules.rule_text: Continuous monitoring of the EudraVigilance database is a legal requirement for all MAHs; monitoring must be conducted at least every 6 months at minimum frequency.
rules.source_quote: Marketing authorisation holders are required to continuously monitor EudraVigilance... monitoring should be conducted at least every 6 months. [IR Art 18]
rules.exceptions: Products under enhanced pharmacovigilance may require more frequent monitoring; the monitoring frequency must be specified in the PSMF.

rules.rule_text: An emerging safety issue (ESI) that could significantly impact the benefit-risk balance of a product must be notified in writing to the competent authority within 3 working days of identification.
rules.source_quote: Emerging safety issues shall be notified to the competent authority in writing within 3 working days of identification.
rules.exceptions: The ESI notification must include all available supporting evidence at the time of notification; additional data can be provided subsequently.

rules.rule_text: When an MAH concludes, after their own analysis, that a validated signal requires further competent authority review, a standalone signal notification must be submitted to the Agency within 30 days of that conclusion.
rules.source_quote: A standalone signal notification shall be submitted to the Agency within 30 days of the marketing authorisation holder concluding that further competent authority analysis is required.
rules.exceptions: Signals that do not require regulatory action may be included in the next PSUR rather than submitted as standalone notifications.

rules.rule_text: For validated signals requiring labeling variation: important risks must result in variation submission within 3 months; non-important risks within 6 months of the PRAC assessment outcome.
rules.source_quote: For validated signals requiring variation: submit within 3 months (important risks) or 6 months (non-important risks) of the PRAC assessment outcome.
rules.exceptions: Extensions to these timelines require agreement with the competent authority and must be documented.

rules.rule_text: PRAC rapporteur or the lead member state must confirm receipt of a signal and confirm whether the signal is validated within 30 days of receiving it from the MAH.
rules.source_quote: The PRAC rapporteur or lead Member State confirms the signal within 30 days of receipt.
rules.exceptions: Complex signals requiring additional data may be subject to extended assessment timelines agreed between PRAC and the MAH.

rules.rule_text: The standard PRAC signal assessment procedure allows 2 months for the MAH to submit additional data and 2 months for PRAC assessment; total standard assessment period is 4 months from signal validation.
rules.source_quote: The standard signal assessment timeframe: 2 months for MAH to submit data + 2 months for PRAC assessment.
rules.exceptions: Urgent safety signals triggering referral procedures are assessed on an accelerated timeline; the specific timeline is defined by the type of referral.

rules.rule_text: Signals tracked in EPITT (European Pharmacovigilance Issues Tracking Tool) must be referenced by their EPITT number in all communications with regulatory authorities.
rules.source_quote: Signals tracked in EPITT shall be referenced by their EPITT number in regulatory communications.
rules.exceptions: Signals not yet entered in EPITT should reference the internal signal number pending EPITT assignment.

rules.rule_text: Refuted signals (signals where assessment concludes no new risk) must not be submitted as standalone notifications; they must be reported only in the next periodic safety update report (PSUR).
rules.source_quote: Refuted signals shall not be subject to standalone notification but should be reported in the next PSUR.
rules.exceptions: If the competent authority specifically requests notification of a refuted signal, the MAH must comply.

rules.rule_text: Foreign manufacturers exporting drugs to Japan must obtain accreditation from the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare before any export can occur; manufacturing and import without accreditation is prohibited.
rules.source_quote: Foreign manufacturers of drugs to be exported to Japan must obtain accreditation from the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare. [PAL Article 13-3]
rules.exceptions: Products manufactured for clinical trial purposes may have different requirements under clinical trial notification procedures.

rules.rule_text: Foreign manufacturer accreditation in Japan is granted per manufacturing category; a separate application is required for each category of products manufactured at the facility.
rules.source_quote: Accreditation is granted per manufacturing category; separate applications are required for each category.
rules.exceptions: Where a facility manufactures products falling into a single broad category, a single application may cover all products in that category.

rules.rule_text: Foreign manufacturer accreditation in Japan is valid for 5 years from the date of grant; renewal using the prescribed form (Form No. 20) must be submitted before expiry.
rules.source_quote: Foreign manufacturer accreditation is valid for 5 years. Renewal must be applied for using Form No. 20 before expiry.
rules.exceptions: Lapse of accreditation requires a new application, not a renewal; products cannot be imported during the lapse period.

rules.rule_text: Any changes to the foreign manufacturer's name, address, representatives, manufacturing facility, or manufacturing category must be notified to the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare within 30 days of the change.
rules.source_quote: Changes to name, address, executives, facility or manufacturing category shall be notified to the Minister within 30 days. [Article 100 PAL Enforcement Regulations]
rules.exceptions: Minor administrative changes (e.g., telephone number) may not require formal notification, but all substantive changes must be reported.

rules.rule_text: The principle for foreign manufacturer accreditation is document examination (not on-site inspection); PMDA assesses applications based on submitted documents without mandatory pre-accreditation inspection.
rules.source_quote: Document examination is the principle for foreign manufacturer accreditation; on-site inspection is not the standard requirement.
rules.exceptions: PMDA or MHLW may conduct on-site GMP inspections in addition to document review, particularly for new or complex manufacturing processes.

rules.rule_text: The Japanese marketing authorization holder (MAH in Japan) may submit the foreign manufacturer accreditation application on behalf of the foreign manufacturer, but the foreign manufacturer's name must appear as the applicant on the application form.
rules.source_quote: The Japanese marketing authorisation holder may submit the application on behalf of the foreign manufacturer, but the foreign manufacturer's name must appear on the form.
rules.exceptions: A power of attorney from the foreign manufacturer to the Japanese MAH must be included with the application.

rules.rule_text: All application documents for foreign manufacturer accreditation must be prepared in Japanese or accompanied by a Japanese translation; documents in other languages without translation will not be accepted.
rules.source_quote: Application documents shall be in Japanese or accompanied by a certified Japanese translation.
rules.exceptions: Certain supporting technical documents may be accepted in English with a Japanese summary at PMDA's discretion.

rules.rule_text: Foreign manufacturers must maintain manufacturing records and quality control records equivalent to Japanese GMP standards; records must be available for review by Japanese authorities on request.
rules.source_quote: Foreign manufacturers must maintain manufacturing and quality control records equivalent to Japanese GMP standards; records shall be available to Japanese authorities upon request.
rules.exceptions: Manufacturers already GMP-certified by ICH-member regulatory authorities may use mutual recognition or reliance mechanisms to satisfy Japanese GMP requirements.

rules.rule_text: Carcinogenicity testing is required for drugs intended for clinical use of 6 months or longer duration, or for drugs intended to be used intermittently throughout a patient's lifetime.
rules.source_quote: がん原性試験は、投与期間が6ヶ月以上の医薬品について実施する。(Carcinogenicity studies are required for drugs with clinical use of 6 months or longer.) [Notification 薬食審査発第1127001号]
rules.exceptions: Anticancer drugs intended for life-threatening diseases may conduct carcinogenicity studies post-approval.

rules.rule_text: The standard carcinogenicity testing battery in Japan is: one long-term rodent study (rat preferred, 24-30 months) plus one short/medium-term in vivo study system.
rules.source_quote: 通常は1種の長期齧歯類試験（ラットが推奨）と1種の短/中期試験を組み合わせる。(Standard: 1 long-term rodent species [rat preferred] + 1 short/medium-term in vivo study system.)
rules.exceptions: Scientific justification may support deviation from this standard battery, e.g., for biological products.

rules.rule_text: Rat carcinogenicity studies must run for 24-30 months; mouse or hamster studies run for 18-24 months. Each dose group must contain at least 50 animals per sex.
rules.source_quote: ラット試験は24〜30ヶ月、マウス・ハムスターは18〜24ヶ月とし、各群50匹以上/性とする。(Rat: 24-30 months; Mouse/Hamster: 18-24 months; ≥50 animals/sex/group.)
rules.exceptions: Transgenic models used as the short/medium-term system may have different group size requirements per the validated protocol.

rules.rule_text: For carcinogenicity studies, 3 dose levels plus a concurrent control group are required; the high dose must be selected to maximize exposure while allowing survival throughout the study.
rules.source_quote: 3用量群と対照群を設定し、高用量は試験期間中の生存を確保しつつ曝露を最大化するよう設定する。(3 dose levels plus control; high dose maximizes exposure while ensuring survival.)
rules.exceptions: For genotoxic substances, long-term carcinogenicity studies are not required; instead, a chronic toxicity study of up to 1 year duration is required.

rules.rule_text: When rodent and human pharmacokinetics are similar, the PK-based high-dose selection criterion requires AUC in rodent to be at least 25 times the human AUC at the maximum clinical dose.
rules.source_quote: げっ歯類と人のPKが類似する場合、げっ歯類AUCは最大臨床用量でのヒトAUCの25倍以上とする。(When rodent/human PK are similar: AUC in rodent ≥25x human AUC.) [Divergence from ICH S1C(R2) which permits MTD as primary criterion]
rules.exceptions: Where AUC 25x is not achievable due to saturation or toxicity, the maximum feasible dose with scientific justification may be used.

rules.rule_text: The limit dose for carcinogenicity studies is 1500 mg/kg/day when the maximum clinical dose does not exceed 500 mg/man/day.
rules.source_quote: 最大臨床用量が500 mg/人/日以下の場合、限度用量は1500 mg/kg/日とする。(Limit dose: 1500 mg/kg/day when max clinical dose ≤500 mg/man/day.)
rules.exceptions: For drugs with very high maximum clinical doses, the limit dose calculation must be adapted; ICH S1C(R2) guidance applies.

rules.rule_text: For genotoxic substances, long-term carcinogenicity studies are not required; instead, a chronic toxicity study of up to 1 year duration is required.
rules.source_quote: 遺伝毒性物質については、長期がん原性試験は必要なく、1年間までの慢性毒性試験を実施する。(Genotoxic substances: long-term carcinogenicity study not required; chronic toxicity study up to 1 year required.)
rules.exceptions: The competent authority may require a long-term carcinogenicity study for genotoxic substances if there is specific mechanistic concern.

rules.rule_text: In vitro studies evaluating the potential for P-gp and BCRP substrate/inhibitor interaction must be conducted for all investigational drugs by in vitro studies in principle; this applies regardless of route of administration.
rules.source_quote: The possibility of working as a substrate of P-gp and BCRP should be evaluated for all investigational drugs by in vitro studies in principle.
rules.exceptions: Products administered only by routes where P-gp/BCRP are irrelevant (e.g., dermal) may have reduced evaluation with justification.

rules.rule_text: Clinical drug interaction studies must be conducted in compliance with GCP (Standards for the Conduct of Clinical Trials of Medical Products) and pharmacokinetic drug interactions must be evaluated in accordance with Clinical Pharmacokinetic Studies of Pharmaceuticals (PMSB Notification No. 796, June 1, 2001).
rules.source_quote: Clinical drug interaction studies should be conducted in compliance with Good Clinical Practice (Standards for the Conduct of Clinical Trials of Medical Products) (GCP), and pharmacokinetic drug interactions should be evaluated in accordance with 'Clinical Pharmacokinetic Studies on Pharmaceuticals.' (Notification No. 796 of the Evaluation and Licensing Division, PMSB, dated June 1, 2001)
rules.exceptions: Post-marketing interaction studies may follow modified designs based on real-world clinical needs.

rules.rule_text: When the contribution ratio (CR) of a metabolic pathway controlled by a drug-metabolizing enzyme to the overall elimination is estimated at 25% or more, implementation of clinical drug interaction studies using drugs that affect the enzyme must be considered.
rules.source_quote: If the CR of the elimination pathway controlled by a certain drug metabolizing enzyme to the overall elimination of the investigational drug is estimated to be 25% or more, implementation of clinical drug interaction studies using drugs that affect the enzyme in question should be considered.
rules.exceptions: If a strong inhibitor study shows no clinically meaningful interaction, additional inhibitor studies for the same enzyme may not be required.

rules.rule_text: Investigational drugs mainly eliminated via hepatic metabolism or biliary excretion (either route accounting for 25% or more of total clearance) must be assessed as potential substrates for hepatic uptake transporters OATP1B1 and OATP1B3.
rules.source_quote: Investigational drugs which are mainly eliminated via hepatic metabolism or biliary excretion (i.e., clearance via either route accounting for 25% or more of the total clearance) should be assessed to examine whether they are substrates for the hepatic uptake transporters OATP1B1 and 1B3.
rules.exceptions: Products that do not undergo significant hepatic processing may be exempt from OATP assessment with scientific justification.

rules.rule_text: Investigational drugs whose major route of elimination is active renal secretion (renal secretion clearance accounting for 25% or more of total clearance) must be assessed as potential substrates of OAT1, OAT3, OCT2, MATE1, or MATE2-K.
rules.source_quote: Investigational drugs whose major route of elimination is active renal secretion (renal secretion clearance accounting for 25% or more of the total clearance), should be assessed to examine whether they are substrates of OAT1, OAT3, OCT2, MATE1 or MATE2-K.
rules.exceptions: Drugs primarily eliminated by glomerular filtration without active tubular secretion may be exempt from renal transporter substrate assessment.

rules.rule_text: If the investigational drug shows high binding ratios to plasma proteins (90% or higher) in vitro, the species of binding protein and the degree of binding must be identified; protein binding displacement interaction must be evaluated when the drug has a plasma protein binding ratio of about 99% or higher, a narrow therapeutic range, and specific elimination characteristics.
rules.source_quote: If the investigational drug shows high binding ratios to plasma proteins (90% or higher) in vitro, it is necessary to identify the species of binding protein and the degree of binding. Significant drug interactions may occur between the investigational drug and concomitant drugs that strongly bind to plasma proteins in the cases of the investigational drug which has a plasma protein binding ratio of about 99% or higher, a narrow therapeutic range.
rules.exceptions: Displacement-only interactions without changes in AUC of unbound drug are rarely clinically significant; clinical relevance must be assessed.

rules.rule_text: Drug interaction studies must address drug interactions from both aspects: the investigational drug as the 'affected drug' (affected by concomitant drugs) and as the 'interacting drug' (affecting concomitant drugs); both directions must be evaluated in the drug interaction development program.
rules.source_quote: Drug interactions between a drug under development (investigational drug) and approved drugs that may be used with the investigational drug should be studied from the two aspects, i.e., the case where the investigational drug is the 'affected drug'... and the case where the investigational drug is the 'interacting drug'.
rules.exceptions: For biological products where PK-mediated DDI potential is known to be limited, a reduced DDI evaluation may be accepted with justification.

rules.rule_text: In vitro studies evaluating the possibility of the investigational drug inhibiting P450 enzymes must be carried out for all major isoenzymes: CYP1A2, CYP2B6, CYP2C8, CYP2C9, CYP2C19, CYP2D6, and CYP3A; major metabolites should also be evaluated for inhibition potential.
rules.source_quote: In vitro metabolism studies should be carried out to determine whether the investigational drug exerts an inhibitory effect on P450. This section mainly discusses the possibility of drug interaction mediated by major primary P450 isoenzymes, CYP1A2, CYP2B6, CYP2C8, CYP2C9, CYP2C19, CYP2D6, and CYP3A (CYP3A4 and CYP3A5).
rules.exceptions: For highly selective compounds with no structural features suggesting P450 interaction, a limited panel may be justified.

rules.rule_text: For cytokines or cytokine modifiers, the need for clinical drug interaction studies to evaluate effects on CYP450 or transporters must be considered, due to the known potential of cytokines to modulate P450 expression.
rules.source_quote: If the investigational drug is a cytokine or a cytokine modifier, the need for implementation of clinical drug interaction studies to evaluate the effects of the investigational drug on P450 or transporter should be considered.
rules.exceptions: Biological products eliminated through receptor-mediated internalization with no expected P450 modulation may have reduced DDI evaluation.

rules.rule_text: Drug interaction information including results of in vitro studies and clinical studies must be reflected in drug package inserts under the INTERACTIONS section; the criteria for inclusion and the level of detail required are specified in the PMDA package insert guidelines.
rules.source_section: 8. General Principles for Package Insert Labeling
rules.source_quote: General principles for reflection of information and alerts on drug interaction in package inserts... Description in Precautions in drug package inserts; Description in 'Drugs' in the section of 'INTERACTIONS'.
rules.exceptions: Information from studies conducted post-approval must be included in the next package insert revision submitted to PMDA.
